stewart_read: Rep opens with a bare name-check — no company identification, no legitimacy framing, no reference to why the call is happening. The codex lists 'clear_company_introduction' as a required success signal for every intro variant. On a hostile customer who has already decided 'no,' the absence of legitimacy framing in the first sentence removes the only tool that could have interrupted Urban's autopilot before he restated his refusal.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have intended to establish identity first and follow with company framing — a two-step open. Rejected: Urban's immediate refusal at 00:07 consumed the space where company framing would have landed, and the rep never circled back to it.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the intros.standard success signals list. The company introduction is not optional on a hostile open — it is the legitimacy anchor that gives the customer a reason to stay on the line. Drill the full intro sequence so it fires before the customer can restate a prior objection.

stewart_read: Urban's opening line is a textbook hello_objection — a refusal in the first 30 seconds before any pitch has been made. The codex prescribes a specific procedure: acknowledge, then make a small ask ('Can I have just 30 seconds to tell you why I'm calling, and if it's not relevant, I'll leave you alone?'). The rep skipped this entirely and moved to diagnostic questioning ('What did they tell you?'). The diagnostic question happened to keep Urban talking, but it did so by entering Urban's frame rather than establishing a new one — a reactive recovery, not a protocol execution.
alternative_hypothesis: The diagnostic question could be read as a variant of the small-ask — asking for permission to understand the situation. Rejected: the small-ask protocol is designed to give the customer a sense of control before the rep speaks; the diagnostic question instead asks the customer to do work (explain the prior call), which is a different dynamic.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the hello_objection protocol verbatim. The small-ask is a one-sentence procedure that takes three seconds — there is no reason to improvise around it. On a hostile open, the protocol exists precisely because improvisation tends to enter the customer's frame rather than interrupt it.

quote: Yeah. I mean, isn't isn't that less than what your average bill is?
stewart_read: Urban has handed the rep his exact bill number ($138) and the competing quote ($127). The rep's response is a math-check — 'isn't that less?' — which is technically correct but emotionally flat. The codex's objection_inversion move was available here: flip Urban's $11 savings framing into the cost of staying ('What you can't afford is your current bill'). Instead the rep validated Urban's frame by accepting the $11 delta as the unit of analysis. Urban's response ('Yeah. $11. That that's not worth it to me.') confirms the frame was not interrupted.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been setting up the end-date argument (01:17) as a two-step move — establish the math first, then introduce the payoff horizon. Rejected: the end-date argument at 01:17 is not connected back to Urban's $138 bill or the $11 delta, so the two-step never closes.
coaching_implication: This is the highest-leverage missed move in the call. Use the objection_inversion framework with this exact exchange: Urban's numbers are already on the table. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'Right — and what happens to that $138 in year 10? Year 20? The solar payment ends. The utility bill doesn't.' No fabricated numbers needed — Urban's own $138 is the leverage. Drill the inversion move until it fires automatically when a customer quantifies their savings dismissal.

quote: Well, did you know that I don't know if you are aware, but solar actually has an end date. So once your panels are paid off, then you're you don't have an electric or a solar payment at all because you're just generating your own energy.
stewart_read: Rep pivots to a feature explanation — the payoff end-date — without anchoring it to Urban's stated concern. The codex flags 'explaining panel technology' and 'listing environmental benefits' as anti-patterns under curiosity_over_features; the end-date explanation is the same category of move. The rep is selling a feature rather than making Urban curious about his own specific math. The explanation also arrives after Urban has already re-entrenched (01:08), so it lands as a sales pitch rather than a discovery.
alternative_hypothesis: The end-date argument is substantively correct and could have been the right move if delivered as a question rather than an explanation — 'Did anyone show you what happens to your payment after the panels are paid off?' Rejected as a strategic read: the rep's delivery ('did you know... I don't know if you are aware') is explanatory framing, not curiosity creation.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the curiosity_over_features anti-pattern list. The end-date insight is real leverage — but it needs to be delivered as a question that makes Urban want to know his own number, not as a feature the rep explains. Suggested rephrase intent: turn the end-date into a question Urban hasn't been asked yet, not a fact the rep volunteers.

quote: Hey. The lie was that the the Virginia Power was putting them up there for free, which I know is not true.
stewart_read: Urban introduces a specific claim about a Dominion Energy program he saw on a website — a program he believed offered free panels. The rep's response ('Virginia Power, I don't think does panels for free') is a partial engagement with a closer-tier topic. The rep correctly does not fabricate a counter-claim, but also does not use the universal escape hatch ('that's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer') to redirect. The moment passes without either resolving Urban's misinformation or routing it to the closer.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been trying to validate Urban's skepticism ('you're right, that sounds off') as a trust-building move before redirecting. Rejected: the rep's hedge ('I don't think') signals uncertainty rather than validation, and no redirect follows.
coaching_implication: When a customer references a specific competitor program or government claim the rep cannot verify, the setter_scope_discipline escape hatch is the correct move. The rep should not engage the claim on its merits — that is closer-tier content. Drill: 'I can't speak to what Dominion is running, but our specialist can compare whatever you've seen against what we'd put together for your home specifically.'

quote: And and the program broke for twenty five years. That's $30,000. No way I'm paying that.
stewart_read: Urban has now done the rep's math for them — 25 years × $127/month ≈ $38,100, which he rounds to $30,000. This is the second missed inversion opportunity in the call. Urban's own arithmetic is the leverage: his $138/month utility bill over 25 years = $41,400 with no end date, versus a solar payment that terminates at payoff. The rep had already introduced the end-date concept at 01:17 but never connected it to Urban's 25-year frame. The inversion was available and unused for the second time.
alternative_hypothesis: By this point Urban has issued a removal request ('you could just cross me off, please') at 02:05, so the strategic question is whether attempting the inversion here would have been productive or would have escalated. The strategic-exit read — that the rep correctly read Urban as past the point of recovery — is better supported by the explicit removal request that follows 10 seconds later. However, the missed inversion at 01:55 is still a coaching signal: if the rep had deployed the inversion at 01:08 (the first opportunity), Urban may not have reached the 25-year frame at all.
coaching_implication: Use this moment in the 1-on-1 to show the rep that Urban's own math was the weapon — and the rep never picked it up. The $30,000 vs. $41,400 comparison (Urban's $138 × 25 years) requires no fabricated numbers. The coaching point is not 'you should have saved this call at 01:55' — it is 'the inversion at 01:08 was the moment; by 01:55 the window had closed.' Sequence matters.

quote: I might as well pay it out of my pocket upfront and then don't even have to worry about it. But I'm just gonna say, you could just cross me off, please.
stewart_read: Urban's removal request is a hard procedural close — not an objection, not a soft no. The rep's response ('Okay. No problem. You have yourself a good day, Irvin.') correctly honors the request without argument. This is the one clean execution in the call: the rep did not attempt a last-ditch pitch, did not argue, did not create a negative final impression. The strategic-exit read holds here — honoring the removal request is the right move.
alternative_hypothesis: A reactive read would flag this as a missed final attempt. Rejected: Urban has stated his position three times in escalating form and issued an explicit removal request. Any further pitch attempt would have been a protocol violation and a relationship cost with no realistic upside.
coaching_implication: Acknowledge this to the rep as the one moment they executed correctly. The call was lost earlier — at 00:03 (no legitimacy framing) and 01:02 (no inversion). The clean exit at 02:15 is not a win, but it is not a loss either. Reinforce: when a customer issues a removal request, honor it cleanly. The rep did.
